comments.text: GPT-5.2 Pro believes the statement is invalid in its current form. See its reasoning below: --- As stated it has a quantifier bug: it says "for any $\epsilon>0$, there exist $M=M(\epsilon)$ and $B \subset \{N+1,\ldots,M\}$ …". But $N$ is arbitrary, so if I take $N > M(\epsilon)$ then $\{N+1,\ldots,M\} = \varnothing$ and you can't add anything. Then the conclusion $|A \cup B| \ge (1-\epsilon)M^{1/2}$ fails, e.g., for $A = \{1\}$ when $M$ is large. So $M$ must depend on $N$ (or on $A$), i.e., "for all $N$ and all Sidon $A \subset [1,N]$ there exists some $M > M_0(A,\epsilon)$ and $B \subset (N,M]$ …". With the broken quantifiers, the statement is trivially false.

Yes, $M$ should depend on both $N$ and $A$ also. This is implicit (in general, by convention, existential variables introduced after universal variables are assumed to depend on them in an unspecified manner). I added the $M(\epsilon)$ to especially highlight that it also depends on $\epsilon$, but do see that this might lead to confusion, so I'll edit it.
I'd call this a "quantifier bug" in GPT-5.2 Pro!

I believe GPT-5.2 Pro is providing value here and is not shown to be in possession of a "bug". Also we don't know what question Gusarich asks. Maybe they asked if there is a trivial resolution to the problem or something like that.
